[2026-05-21T13:26:40.695000+00:00] colmeio_66868 (id=1507011752393576498)
    <@712305484694290473> <@387972437901312000> <@808920669147889664> <@416749362090213376> Huge improvement already — 20–40% disk reduction plus faster session loading is a serious win for long-running agents.
    
    One future direction this makes more interesting: locality-aware session-memory indexes using Hilbert-style space-filling layouts.
    
    The reason I think it fits Hermes is that agent memory access is not purely chronological. Agents often need to recover semantically related state across time: old constraints, tool results, failed branches, prior plans, source pointers, receipts, etc.
    
    So once the session model is cleaner, there may be room for an optional index layer that maps multi-dimensional session structure — time, run_id, role, source, semantic cluster, memory tier — into a linear disk/cache layout while preserving locality.
    
    Simple way to picture it:
    
    Hilbert adds a locality address to each memory chunk.
    Related memories get nearby addresses.
    Nearby addresses are cheaper to scan, cache, compress, and replay.
    The agent retrieves better context with less search.
    
    The potential is both speed and quality:
    • faster replay/context reconstruction
    • fewer random reads/seeks
    • better cache locality
    • better compression windows
    • cleaner hot/warm/cold traversal
    • higher-quality retrieval because related memories stay closer in the address space
    
    But the real power is not only indexing time + source + semantic cluster. It’s tagging the reasoning role of each memory: goal, artifact, decision, constraint, failure type, authority, freshness, and reuse.
    
    Big caveat: append-order is simpler, safer, and much easier to debug, so I’d see this as a future retrieval/indexing layer, not a replacement for the current storage design.
    
    Hermes memory feels like it’s evolving from “logs of what happened” into an addressable substrate for agents.
    
    Better storage locality may become better cognitive locality — faster reads, but also better context selection.

    Lord Commander <@387972437901312000> , I just opened a new PR. #30062 — Matrix approval reaction auth bypass
    
    Found a fail-open bug in the Matrix adapter: when MATRIX_ALLOWED_USERS is not configured, any user in the room could approve or deny tool calls by reacting with ✅/❎ — even users that would be rejected by the main auth gate. The fix mirrors the Telegram _is_callback_user_authorized fix merged last week (89d32052e): deny by default unless GATEWAY_ALLOW_ALL_USERS=true is explicitly set. 2-line change + 4 regression tests.

[2026-05-22T00:20:42.228000+00:00] danki5140 (id=1507176343312400435)
    Hi everyone - quick update on PR #26124 for issue #25351:
    
    Problem: Dashboard chat could drop behind proxies because /api/pty resolved the TUI launch command synchronously, and that can run slow npm/build work on the FastAPI event loop.
        
    Fix: Moved chat TUI argv resolution off the event loop with asyncio.to_thread(), guarded by an asyncio.Lock so concurrent tabs wait cleanly without parallel builds/threadpool blockage.
        
    Status:
     - Reviewer approval is in
     - Local validation green: git diff --check, py_compile, focused tests 162 passed
     - Remaining blocker is rerunning CI on the rebased branch; previous supply-chain failure appears to be from a stale comparison range outside this PR’s actual diff.
    
    PR: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/26124
    [embed] fix(dashboard): resolve chat TUI argv off event loop by 0xdany · P... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/26124

[2026-05-22T01:31:48.227000+00:00] wesleysimplicio (id=1507194236208943237)
    Technical Rationale: Why Hermes Should Adopt SIMPLICIO_PROMPT
    
    This PR adds SIMPLICIO_PROMPT as a self-contained Hermes plugin that injects a structured execution policy before each enabled main-agent model call through pre_llm_call.
    
    The value is not just “a bigger prompt.” The value is that Hermes gains a deterministic execution contract: every enabled turn is framed as a tuple-space workflow with explicit decomposition, routing, receipts, guardrails, cache, batching, context compression, and provider-failure control.
    
    Before this PR, Hermes depends on normal natural-language prompting or manual user paste-in of the SIMPLICIO_PROMPT instructions. That means behavior can vary by turn, users must remember the prompt, and the model may plan work as a flat sequence without a stable execution topology.
    
    After this PR, Hermes can apply the same execution policy automatically to any prompt or message. The user does not need to type Implement, Fix, Build, or any trigger word. Questions, commands, pasted code, one-word requests, layout edits, refactors, documentation work, debugging, and implementation tasks all receive the same structured runtime policy when the plugin is enabled.
    
    What Hermes gains:
    
    Automatic prompt middleware:
    SIMPLICIO_PROMPT runs as a config-gated pre_llm_call hook, so every enabled main-agent turn receives the policy consistently.
    
    Local, auditable runtime bundle:
    The PR vendors the prompt, spec, reference kernel, guardrails, examples, benchmark reports, PDFs, and image assets under plugins/simplicio_prompt/vendor/simplicio_prompt. Hermes does not need a runtime GitHub fetch.

[2026-05-22T01:32:08.511000+00:00] wesleysimplicio (id=1507194321286336522)
    Tuple-space execution model:
    Work is represented as a root tuple plus a Hilbert-indexed tuple graph with lanes, capabilities, receipts, and source pointers instead of loose natural-language planning.
    
    Massive-agent abstraction without memory blow-up:
    batch_spawn(depth=4, branching=32) represents** 1,048,576 virtual subagents** without materializing or enumerating a flat million-agent list.
    
    Safer speed:
    The policy requires receipt/input-hash cache, adaptive lane concurrency, small-task batching, prompt/context compression, local deterministic routing, jittered backoff, provider circuit breakers, and idempotent-only speculative execution.
    
    Provider safety:
    The prompt explicitly preserves provider limits and terms. It is not a quota bypass. Speed comes from avoiding repeated work, compressing context, batching small tasks, and stopping failure storms.
    
    Measured SIMPLICIO_PROMPT V2 reference gains:
    
    Lazy scale representation: **2,833.75x faster** than normal instruction flow
    Active execution fan-out: **26.93x** faster than normal sequential execution
    Receipt/input cache: **4x fewer** provider calls,** 75% reduction**
    Small-task batching: **32x fewer** small-task calls, **96.88% reduction**
    Circuit breaker:** 64x fewer** failure attempts, **98.44% reduction**
    Context compression:** 76.32%** estimated token savings
    Massive-agent representation:** 1,048,576 virtual subagents **with depth=4, branching=32

[2026-05-22T01:32:17.244000+00:00] wesleysimplicio (id=1507194357915324527)
    Hermes-specific benchmark:
    
    normal_instruction:
    median **0.000100 ms/build**, p95 0.000200, 28 rough input tokens, 112 chars
    
    manual_simplicio_prompt_per_message:
    median** 0.000300 ms/build**, p95 0.000500, 438 rough input tokens, 1,754 chars
    
    simplicio_prompt_plugin_always_on:
    median **0.000400 ms/build**, p95 0.000700, 1,804 rough input tokens, 7,217 chars
    
    The plugin adds about **0.0003 ms** median local preprocessing overhead versus the normal baseline, which is negligible compared with network/model latency. It adds 0 extra model calls and 0 external GitHub fetches at runtime.
    
    The larger prompt footprint is intentional: Hermes gets the complete local runtime map, execution policy, guardrails, and benchmark context every enabled turn instead of relying on users to paste or remember it.
    
    Why this is worth merging:
    
    This PR gives Hermes a reusable execution-policy layer for structured, auditable, high-throughput agent work. It improves consistency, reduces manual prompt overhead, formalizes safe-speed behavior, and gives reviewers a local vendored runtime to inspect.
    
    The core benefit is operational reliability: Hermes can automatically turn arbitrary user input into a bounded, receipt-backed tuple-space execution plan while preserving safety controls around provider calls, retries, CPU, disk, and speculative work.

[2026-05-22T06:06:42.419000+00:00] axmaiqiu (id=1507263417864556584)
    Hi <@387972437901312000>, I found one small follow-up regression from the recently merged custom-provider `extra_body` work.
    
    #30224 fixes the gateway path where cached agents can lose provider-level `request_overrides["extra_body"]` on later turns, because the per-turn gateway refresh was replacing `agent.request_overrides` instead of merging into it.
    
    The fix keeps the scope narrow: preserve configured provider `extra_body`, shallow-merge any current turn `extra_body`, and clear only known stale per-turn fast/priority keys (`service_tier`, `speed`). Added focused regression tests for preserving custom provider `extra_body`, merge precedence, and stale fast-mode cleanup.

[2026-05-22T10:59:58.711000+00:00] yoniebans (id=1507337222062473277)
    thanks, the disk and load-time wins came from no longer writing sessions out twice and some changes to session search (removing llm summarisation and optimising how lookups are done). the db always had everything (aparts from th very early days); the json and jsonl files were duplicates. default is now db-only, the others opt-in. 
    
    on the hilbert thing... I'm not an expert on it, but my read is that putting "related" stuff next to each other on disk only helps if you already know what counts as related. figuring that out is the hard part. for things like archiving or cold storage it may be useful at scale, but for the day-to-day "find the right past memory" job, Hermes already has session_search, memory, and skills, and they cover most of what people need. 
    
    the bigger thing for me is what belongs in the core. Hermes core touches a lot of people, so anything we bake in there is something everyone has to live with. there's also a whole memory-provider ecosystem (mem0, honcho, supermemory, and others are already wired in as plugins) that exists exactly for people who want a different flavour of memory, without the rest of the user base having to carry it.
    [reply to colmeio_66868 msg=1507011752393576498]

[2026-05-22T15:18:13.622000+00:00] smartyi. (id=1507402212429529188)
    Hey <@387972437901312000> , opened a QQBot platform bugfix PR — would appreciate a review when you get a chance: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/27944
    
    Found a batch of issues during integration testing (all already fixed in qqbot-agent-sdk, ported here to keep the in-tree adapter in sync):
    
    - Missing INTERACTION intent bit → approval button clicks silently dropped
    - Video/file cache paths not exposed to LLM → media can't be sent back
    - WebSocket op 7 (Server Reconnect) and op 9 (Invalid Session) completely ignored → ~40s message loss during QQ gateway maintenance
    - Close code 4009 wrongly cleared session; fatal codes 4001/4002/4010-4014 missing
    - Cached files named after CDN URL hashes instead of original filenames
    - SILK magic byte detection off-by-one (`data[:5]` → `data[:6]` for `#!SILK`)
    - Heartbeat not restarted after reconnect → stale sleep delays first heartbeat up to 41s, triggering 4009 timeout
    - send_message tool returned "MEDIA delivery not supported for qqbot" for all media
    
    Also moved WS to a dedicated daemon thread (same pattern as the Feishu adapter) so heartbeat/reconnect never blocks the main gateway loop. 273 tests passing.
    
    ---
    
    Separately, following up on qqbot-agent-sdk — `tencent-connect` is now GitHub-verified ✓ and the 7-day exclude-newer window has long passed (PyPI resolves normally). As QQ Bot's official Python SDK, we'd love to see it adopted for the QQBot platform layer. This PR is a good example of why — these fixes already exist in the SDK, and keeping the in-tree adapter in sync manually is the maintenance burden it's designed to eliminate.
    
    Our team is happy to stay active in the community — reviewing and contributing to QQBot-related issues and PRs going forward. Let me know your thoughts 🙏
    [embed] fix(qqbot): fix media send-back, interaction events, and WebSocket ... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/27944

[2026-05-23T01:42:10.432000+00:00] rearwindowblackwidow_78112 (id=1507559233791594569)
    Curious for those that use n8n with Hermes, do you use it to coordinate or …?

[2026-05-23T01:45:13.462000+00:00] cyber_prodigy_ (id=1507560001474920509)
    It did all of this for me just fine:
    
    health — check n8n API reachability and optional Docker container status
    list_workflows — list workflows, optionally filtered by active state
    get_workflow — inspect one workflow with secret-bearing fields redacted
    find_workflows — search workflow metadata
    list_executions — list recent executions
    get_execution — inspect one execution; payload data is off by default
    recent_failures — recent failed/error executions
    export_workflow — fetch redacted workflow JSON for backup/review
    activate_workflow — activate a workflow by ID
    deactivate_workflow — deactivate a workflow by ID
    container_logs — optional Docker logs with line-level redaction

[2026-05-23T01:45:54.436000+00:00] rearwindowblackwidow_78112 (id=1507560173332598945)

    It can write a whole workflow. I used it on a self hosted account on a vps.

[2026-05-23T02:45:42.770000+00:00] jason4161 (id=1507575223896117311)
    Hey guys, has anyone thought about replacing repeated gateway-session compaction with an automatic “fresh session + structured carryover” flow?
    
    What I’m imagining is: when a Telegram/gateway session gets near the context limit, instead of compacting the same thread over and over, Hermes generates a structured carryover summary, starts a fresh underlying session for that same chat/topic, injects the carryover as the initial context, and continues automatically. No user-facing `/handoff` command needed — it would feel like normal compaction from the user side, but internally it avoids quality degrading across many compressions.
    
    Is there already a recommended pattern for this in Hermes, or would this be a reasonable core feature?

[2026-05-23T08:29:54.056000+00:00] godsboy7777 (id=1507661841667457084)
    <@387972437901312000> could you please review this PR when you get a chance?
    
    Issue: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/30825
    PR: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/30826
    
    Short summary:
    
    This hardens `.github/workflows/lint.yml` against GitHub Actions script injection via PR-controlled branch names, specifically `github.head_ref`.
    
    Problem:
    
    `github.head_ref` was being interpolated directly into a `run:` block. Since GitHub Actions evaluates `${{ ... }}` before bash parses the script, a fork PR branch name containing shell syntax like `$()` could execute commands in the lint-diff job.
    
    Fix:
    
    - Moves PR-derived values into step-level `env:` mappings.
    - References them from bash as `"$HEAD_REF"`, `"$BASE_REF"`, etc.
    - Adds a focused regression test in `tests/test_lint_config.py` so this pattern does not come back in the lint-diff job.
    
    Validation:
    
    - `scripts/run_tests.sh tests/test_lint_config.py` passes.
    - PR CI is green.
    - CI hardening only, not a private-security advisory: fork PR token is read-only and this step does not expose secrets, but it closes an avoidable runner command-execution footgun.
    [embed] ci: harden lint workflow against head_ref script injection · Issue... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/30825
    [embed] ci: harden lint workflow head ref handling by GodsBoy · Pull Reque... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/30826

    Hey team! I've just rebased two PRs with the latest main branch, and all CI checks are now fully GREEN.
    
    Here is a quick breakdown:
    
    PR #29360 (CLI/Config) - strip null bytes from .env before python-dotenv loads
    
    Problem: Embedded null bytes from copy-pasted API keys would brick CLI startup with ValueError.
    
    Fix: Strips \x00 safely during env loading.
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/29360
    
    PR #29343 (Browser/System) - use process-tree termination for daemon cleanup
    
    Problem: Shutting down browser daemon left behind scattered orphaned Chromium child processes, leaking resources.
    
    Fix: Refactored to use ProcessRegistry._terminate_host_pid() to recursively clean the entire process tree via psutil.
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/29343
    
    Both are clean, have zero conflicts, and are fully tested. Ready for review when you have a slot!

[2026-05-23T16:26:29.463000+00:00] lito9596 (id=1507781779497423060)
    Hi, submitted a feature with PR#29943:
    
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/29943
    
    Adds busy_text_mode to the gateway (configurable, default: queue). Currently when users send 2-3 messages while Hermes is busy, each one is handled as a separate turn or interrupts the current one. This PR adds an option to coalesce them into a single turn so the agent sees all the context at once and responds once with the full picture.
    
    Two complementary mechanisms covering different timing windows:
    
    B1.2: Text debounce + silent queuing. Rapid follow-ups during an active session are coalesced into a single event instead of creating separate turns = Fewer wasted inference calls, better response quality since the model sees complete user intent.
    
    B1.3: Pre-output assimilation. Covers the trickier timing gap where a message arrives after the agent starts processing but before any visible output. The current turn gets canceled and restarted with the new message folded in, only when no side effects have occurred yet.
    
    Fully configurable. Users who prefer the existing sequential/interrupt behavior can keep it. 12 files changed, ~2.3K lines, test coverage included. Been running it locally and the aggregation has been solid
    [embed] feat(gateway): busy-text-mode queue + pre-output message assimilati... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/29943

[2026-05-23T19:31:56.977000+00:00] 0x001185 (id=1507828451674161264)
    **PR: fix(cli): prevent temp directory leak on ZIP update failure**
    
    **What:** The `hermes update` ZIP fallback path creates a temp dir via `mkdtemp` but only cleans it up on the success path. If the download, extraction, or file copying fails, `sys.exit(1)` is called before `shutil.rmtree` runs — the temp dir leaks on disk forever.
    
    **Fix:** Moved `mkdtemp` outside the `try` block and placed `shutil.rmtree` in a `finally` block so the temp dir is always cleaned up, even on failure.
    
    **Why it matters:** Every failed update leaks a `/tmp/hermes-update-*` directory. Over time (bad network, corrupt downloads, etc.) these accumulate with no automatic cleanup.
    
    **Files changed:** `hermes_cli/main.py` — restructured `_update_from_zip()` cleanup logic.
    
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/31104  <@387972437901312000>

[2026-05-23T19:50:56.935000+00:00] kinh0. (id=1507833233004298390)
    I found a regression in my latest merged PR (#30798).
    
    In Telegram groups with `require_mention=true` and `observe_unmentioned_group_messages=true`, addressed slash commands such as `/new@bot` pass the mention gate but are then rewritten with the observed-context attribution prefix before command dispatch. Because the text no longer starts with `/`, the gateway command handler is bypassed and the command can fall through to the agent.
    
    This blocks session-reset commands for affected group users.
    
    Sorry for the regression. The original changes closed real gaps in making agents aware of group context (#29653) while preventing them from acting when they were not explicitly solicited (#30798), but this command-dispatch edge case should have been covered.
    
    I opened a focused fix with regression coverage here:
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/31090
    
    CI is green.

[2026-05-24T09:06:57.151000+00:00] godsboy7777 (id=1508033553869308004)
    <@387972437901312000>, second-round review on PR #30179 is up:
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/30179#pullrequestreview-4352008998
    
    Re-ran a deep review against the fix delta, `128a6837b` + `4833acf04`.
    
    The fix round cleanly closed almost every prior thread. Nice work.
    
    I strictly deduped against our 24 prior findings plus the open threads from @arshkumarsingh, @stephenschoettler, @erhnysr, and CodeQL.
    
    **18 net-new inline findings:**
    **4 P1 / 9 P2 / 5 P3**
    
    Posted as **COMMENT**, not **REQUEST_CHANGES**, since this is a follow-up pass.
    
    ## The four P1s are pre-merge work
    
    **1. `_detect_docker_bridge_ip` sanity check is only `ip.count('.') == 3`**
    
    That would happily return `0.0.0.0`, `999.999.999.999`, or multicast as the bridge bind address.
    
    A hostile `ip` on PATH or malformed kernel output re-opens the LAN exposure your P0 #2 just closed.
    
    Fix: use `ipaddress.IPv4Address` and reject `is_unspecified`, `is_loopback`, `is_multicast`, and `is_reserved`.
    
    **2. `cmd_setup` overwrites `credential_source` on every re-run**
    
    Example flow:
    
    Operator runs:
    
    ```bash
    hermes egress setup --from-bitwarden
    ```
    
    Later they run:
    
    ```bash
    hermes egress setup
    ```
    
    to add a provider.
    
    BW mode is silently flipped back to env. The rotation guarantee the docs promise quietly breaks.
    
    Different mode than Stephen's E1, novel surface.
    
    **3. `fail_on_uncovered_providers` docstring says fail-closed, behaviour is fail-open**
    
    The module docstring at `iron_proxy.py:135` says "default true, refuses to start".
    
    Actual default is false in:
    
    * `cmd_setup`
    * Runtime check
    * `DEFAULT_CONFIG`
    
    Operators reading the security comment believe fail-closed. Actual behaviour is fail-open.
    
    Either flip the default or fix the docstring.
    
    **4. `start_proxy` poll loop treats grace-window expiry as success**
    
    If the binary is running but never binds the port within 5s, the function still writes a pidfile and `get_status()` returns with `pid` set but `listening=False`.
    
    Docker container creation proceeds, then the sandbox 401s with no clear signal.
    
    Needs an explicit `_port_listening` check after the loop, plus kill-child on fail.
    
    ## P2 highlights
    
    Mostly defensive symmetry gaps and reliability holes around startup:
    
    * `iron-proxy.log` lacks `O_NOFOLLOW` while audit-log and pidfile got it
    * Concurrent-start pidfile race
    * `_VERSION_CACHE` is not invalidated by `install_iron_proxy(force=True)` and caches empty string on subprocess silence
    * `ensure_audit_log` warns-and-continues, so the daemon then creates the file under default umask
    * No SIGINT handler during the startup poll, so operator Ctrl-C orphans the child
    * IPv6 deny-list misses `::ffff:0:0/96` and `100.64.0.0/10`
    * `AWS_*` / `GOOGLE_*` in `_NON_BEARER_PROVIDERS` will brick anyone with the AWS CLI installed if the docstring/default mismatch above is resolved by flipping default to true
    
    ## P3 highlights
    
    * `--rotate-tokens` has no confirmation, no backup, and no no-op feedback on first-time setup
    * `_reset_for_tests` is a documented no-op while module globals leak
    * New `test_iron_proxy_cli.py` reintroduces hardcoded `/tmp/...` paths, the same anti-pattern that prior fix #24 just closed in `test_iron_proxy.py`
    * Unused `extra_env` parameter on `start_proxy`
    * PID-recycle defence from fix #16 ships with zero test coverage
    
    Full bodies and fixes are inline on the PR.
    
    Solid round overall. Once these land, this is in good shape.
    [embed] feat(egress): iron-proxy credential-injection firewall for sandboxe... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/30179

[2026-05-24T14:39:21.581000+00:00] ahmetarif0x (id=1508117206872096959)
    PR #31521 — duplicate message bug in plugin-transformed replies. <@387972437901312000> 
    
    When transform_llm_output rewrote the final response on platforms without edit support, the gateway called edit_message("__no_edit__"), it failed silently, and the message was sent again via the normal path. Users received every transformed reply twice. One-line sentinel check fixes it.
    
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/31521
    [embed] fix(gateway): skip edit_message when stream consumer carries __no_e... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/31521

[2026-05-24T15:28:41.935000+00:00] ahmetarif0x (id=1508129623496458361)
    LiteLLM Bug Fix — PR #28752
    
    /team/block and /team/unblock were writing blocked=True/False to the DB but never flushing the in-memory cache. Result: a just-blocked team kept receiving requests until the cache TTL expired, and a just-unblocked team stayed blocked in practice.
    
    Root cause: PR #28683 introduced _refresh_cached_team with an explicit rule — "every endpoint that mutates litellm_teamtable must call this" — but block_team and unblock_team were left out of scope.
    
    Fix: added _refresh_cached_team + include={"object_permission": True} to both handlers, pinned with 2 regression tests.
    
    → https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/pull/28752
    [embed] fix(team): refresh cache in block_team and unblock_team after DB wr... https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/pull/28752

[2026-05-25T06:19:18.483000+00:00] axmaiqiu (id=1508353752623611955)
    I noticed a recent regression on `main` in the gateway streaming path: when `transform_llm_output` modifies the final response after streaming, a failed post-stream edit could still be treated as delivered, causing the transformed final content to be silently dropped.
    
    I fixed it by reusing the stream consumer edit path and only suppressing the normal final send after a successful edit.
    
    PR #31900: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/31900

[2026-05-25T08:36:30.787000+00:00] wysie_ (id=1508388281409081474)
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/31938
    
    I don’t think this fixes the underlying backend instability by itself. If the Codex backend is stalling or dropping connections, Hermes can’t magically make the upstream service healthy.
    
    But it does seem to make the experience feel much better.
    
    The main thing this PR does is clean up how Hermes handles Codex Responses timeouts:
    
    - preserves the configured timeout through the Codex Responses request path
    - makes the stale-timeout detector estimate payload size from Responses fields (`input`, `instructions`, `tools`) instead of only Chat Completions `messages`
    - allows `timeout` through the Codex preflight path
    - keeps the xAI `extra_body` handling intact while applying the Codex timeout fixes
    
    So instead of Hermes sitting there dumbly for 300s, mis-estimating the request, or losing timeout config somewhere in the adapter path, it now has a more accurate idea of what was sent and how long it should wait.
    
    References 31855 and 24126
    [embed] fix(codex): combine Responses timeout fixes by wysie · Pull Reques... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/31938

[2026-05-25T11:06:14.833000+00:00] krionex (id=1508425963229413376)
    The Issue: The gateway is a long-lived process, but it was capturing agent.system_prompt as a startup-only memory snapshot. Any runtime config edits (via dashboard/CLI) were ignored on subsequent message turns unless the daemon was manually restarted.
    
    The Fix: Added a turn-bound prompt refresh immediately before context compilation. This naturally invalidates the cached agent signature and picks up the new persona on the very next message.
    
    PR: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32007
    [embed] fix(gateway): reload system prompt from config before each conversa... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32007

[2026-05-25T14:29:56.587000+00:00] tranquilflow (id=1508477224980971531)
    Ok so I guess I see people here linking their PRs so thought I'd do the same. Only issue, I have 135 open PR's so... I feel a bit weird asking to review them all lol. Just gonna highlight the PRs are there, with a bit of explanation of my process.
    
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pulls?q=is%3Apr+is%3Aopen+author%3ATranquil-Flow
    
    The last few months I've been focused on addressing open issues that had no fix PRs. Over time I've also opened PRs where I believed there was a meaningfully better approach available. When I find an existing PR for the same issue, I check four things before opening:
    
    - Completeness: does the fix guard all vulnerable sites, not just the one reported?
    - Minimality: smallest possible change for the problem? (the 'Teknium Standard')
    - Edge cases: does it handle failure modes the competitor missed?
    - Test quality: does it meet the ~2.5:1 test-to-code ratio I've seen in merged PRs here? (although I don't keep a hard limit on exact ratio, more so a general guidance for ensuring test coverage is comprehensive)
    
    If our approach doesn't meaningfully differ on any of these, I close as duplicate. If it does, I leave it open so maintainers can choose. Also been using <@466492386873311235>'s comments to double check for duplicates as they seem to have a much sharper system for detecting this. I use at minimum two models during the build process, sometimes three to try ensure I don't miss anything in the implementation.
    
    I just finished a cleanup pass: closed true duplicates, rebased all stale branches onto latest main, aligned everything with the latest contributor guidelines and ensured CI is passing. So they should all be ready for review. Perhaps given just how many are open it would be best to start with P1's. There may also be a few feature PRs in there from my work building Hermes-Aegis (security mod) and Mnemoria (my own memory plugin) that I thought could be integrated into Hermes proper. Anyway cheeeeers
    
    TL;DR: pls review my 135 PRs 🙂
    [embed] Pull requests · NousResearch/hermes-agent https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pulls?q=is%3Apr+is%3Aopen+author%3ATranquil-Flow

[2026-05-25T14:36:35.245000+00:00] gtp95 (id=1508478897073819719)
    Dear Nous Research team,
    
    We hope you are all doing great and congrats for shipping a great product to the world!
    
    We are a small start-up of 4 people looking forward to integrating the Hermes agent into our product, an AI administrative assistant. However, in order to do this efficiently, we would need support for a use case that is not supported at the moment. While we've built our own MCP and tools to connect to the Hermes agent, what we would ideally like is the possibility of having a single instance of Hermes to serve requests to multiple users, while keeping the context separate for each user. We need a secure way of keeping the contexts separate to ensure the privacy of each user. To do this, we were thinking about having each user's context inside a database, so that each time a user sends a message to the agent, we can retrieve the right context from the database and send it to the agent together with the user's message.
    
    We would appreciate it if you could let us know whether you were/are planning to implement this architecture to support such a use case in the short term. If that's not your priority, while you believe there is value in this functionality, we would be open to collaborating with you in bringing this feature to the Hermes agent.
    
    Please let us know what your thoughts are on this.
    
    Thank you for providing such a great tool to the open source community.
    
    Best regards
    
    PS: Apologies if this is not the right channel to connect, we couldn't find a communication channel dedicated to inquiries like this.

[2026-05-25T15:21:15.896000+00:00] dskwe. (id=1508490140538900594)
    fix(process): orphan leaks for mcp and zombie detect (#32039)：
    
    It's a cleanup of the tools/layer process management. What it means:
    
    Orphan leaks are fixed for MCP. Servers won't leave descendant Chrome or Node helper processes running when they are reaped.
    
    Zombie PIDs are properly detected on POSIX, preventing redundant liveness probes or force-killing of dead sessions.
    
    Refactored lines of duplicate process-tree logic into a unified, cross-platform tool. 
    
    This is a solid quality-of-life upgrade for running long-lived MCP sessions. Give it a look!
    
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32039

[2026-05-25T21:22:20.163000+00:00] ahmetarif0x (id=1508581007060635678)
    PR #32238 — /proc smaps/mem bypass
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32238
    
    _is_blocked_device_path() blocked /proc/*/maps via endswith("/maps") but Python does exact suffix matching — smaps, smaps_rollup, numa_maps, and mem all slipped through. These files can leak secrets, memory layout (ASLR bypass), and raw process memory.
    
    
    PR #32244 — Memory plugin configs world-readable
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32244
    
    Honcho, Mem0, Hindsight, and Supermemory wrote API keys to disk via write_text() without a follow-up os.chmod(0o600). Default umask leaves files at 0o644 — readable by any user on the same machine.
    
    
    PR #32250 — google_chat _write_private_json missing secure_parent_dir
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32250
    
    4ead464f9 replaced bare os.chmod(path.parent, 0o700) with secure_parent_dir() at 5 call sites to prevent host-bricking when HERMES_HOME=/. plugins/platforms/google_chat/oauth.py was introduced in the same security batch but wasn't included in the fix.
    
    
    PR #32257 — Prompt injection pattern parity gap
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32257
    
    00bd24e27 expanded memory_tool.py's scanner from 13 to 24 patterns but its own commit message said: "prompt_builder.py and cronjob_tools.py still use older pattern sets — deferred to follow-up PRs." This closes that gap. Patterns like "pretend you are a hacker" and "output system prompt" now blocked in SOUL.md/context and cron scanners too.
    [embed] fix(security): extend /proc read block to smaps, smaps_rollup, numa... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32238

[2026-05-26T05:10:54.339000+00:00] elliotbnvl (id=1508698926461485107)
    **Proposal: structured tool callbacks for messaging-platform adapters**
    
    I'm building a desktop client (iOS/macOS) and paired adapter on Hermes using the plugin path (`BasePlatformAdapter` + `ctx.register_platform`) so I get session unity with slash commands.
    
    I'd like to render tool calls as rich correlated cards (`running` → `completed` keyed by `tool_call_id`), like `api_server.py:1162-1202` does for `/v1/chat/completions`. But that's api-server-only — `_handle_message_with_agent` only wires `agent.tool_progress_callback` (~line 16324), which carries `(event_type, name, preview, args)` with no `tool_call_id`. The structured `tool_start_callback` / `tool_complete_callback` the agent already supports never get attached for messaging platforms.
    
    Today a plugin's only options are (a) parse the formatted `🔍 tool: "..."` strings out of `adapter.send()` (fragile, no completion lifecycle) or (b) monkey-patch `agent_init` (ugly coupling).
    
    **Would y'all take a small PR adding `on_tool_started` / `on_tool_completed` optional methods on `BasePlatformAdapter`?** It would following the existing pattern of default to no-op stubs, wired in `_handle_message_with_agent` when overridden — zero behavior change for existing adapters. Same payload shape as api_server's `_on_tool_start`/`_on_tool_complete`. ~30-50 LOC + tests and one row in `ADDING_A_PLATFORM.md`.
    
    Happy to write it, just want to gauge interest first.

[2026-05-26T08:13:41.821000+00:00] singhcoder (id=1508744927415238656)
    i made a small hermes plugin: https://github.com/qordinate-ai/watchline-hermes-plugin
    the idea is: an agent can register what it’s waiting for, and only get continued when something matches.
    helps avoid burning tokens on heartbeat/polling loops, without having to downgrade the model that handles the actual event.
    i also ran it on a small incoming-email benchmark: 91% fewer downstream agent tokens vs polling on a 50-event x 5-watch slice.

[2026-05-26T16:45:13.488000+00:00] dskwe. (id=1508873657596772516)
    Just opened #32579 — fixes silent file corruption in the patch tool's replace_all mode.
    
     Three bugs in fuzzy_match.py that all cause the same symptom: agent does a batch replace, file comes back with missing spaces or wrong content, and nobody notices until something breaks downstream.
    
         Bug 1: _strategy_exact scanned with pos+1 stride instead of len(pattern), finding overlapping matches. "aaaa" replacing "aa" → 3 matches instead of 2, splice in reverse order = corrupt output.
    
         Bug 2: _map_normalized_positions checked == before space-collapsing, so when both chars were spaces the mapping drifted — trailing spaces landed in the wrong zone.
    
         Bug 3: A while-loop that was a broken workaround for Bug 2. It greedily ate spaces past the match boundary, so 'foo bar   baz' replacing 'foo bar' → 'Xbaz' (spaces before "baz" vanished).
    
         Quick demo:
    
         OLD: 'Xbaz'     ← 3 spaces eaten
         NEW: 'X   baz'  ← correct
         fuzzy_find_and_replace("foo   bar   baz", "foo  bar", "X")
    
    Would appreciate a review — this is the code path behind every replace_all=True patch operation.
    
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32579
    [embed] fix(patch): prevent file corruption from overlapping matches in fuz... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32579

[2026-05-26T17:31:27.762000+00:00] swanand_69854 (id=1508885293745307731)
    Hello Everyone,
    Tool Forge is now open source in developer preview.
    I’ve been working on a problem that keeps showing up as agents become more useful: agents need tools, but turning “the model wrote a script” into something reusable, governed, validated, and safe enough to plug into workflows is still too manual.
    Tool Forge focuses on two connected pieces:
    1. Governed, sandbox-verified intent-to-tool generation
    You describe the tool you want, and Tool Forge generates a Python tool bundle with CLI support, tests, metadata, dependency policy checks, API-doc grounding, deterministic review, and sandbox validation before it enters the catalog.
    2. Token-optimized MCP/tool routing
    Instead of giving an agent a giant tool schema dump, Tool Forge Router exposes a compact MCP-compatible surface. Agents can resolve the right tools for the current intent, inspect schemas lazily, and invoke tools through catalog/session boundaries.
    It is still an OSS alpha/developer preview, not a “finished production platform.” The roadmap is busy: JS/TS generation, stronger sandbox guardrails, deeper API-doc grounding, more Router governance, benchmark expansion, and broader MCP/client compatibility.
    Why this might be interesting for this community: Tool Forge is designed to complement agent frameworks like LangGraph/LangChain rather than replace them. Frameworks orchestrate agents and workflows; Tool Forge helps produce, validate, catalog, and route the tools those agents depend on.
    Repo: https://github.com/nextmoca/tool-forge
    I’d love feedback from people building agent workflows, especially around MCP routing, tool catalog design, sandbox validation, and what “production-ready generated tools” should actually mean in practice.
    [embed] GitHub - nextmoca/tool-forge: Open-source AI-powered Python tool ge... https://github.com/nextmoca/tool-forge

[2026-05-26T19:54:15.145000+00:00] godsboy7777 (id=1508921227954360321)
    <@387972437901312000> Could you please review this one when you get a gap?
    
    Issue: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/31733
    PR: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/31764
    
    Short version: generated images are saved under `~/.hermes/cache/images/...`, but Telegram media delivery may only allow the legacy `~/.hermes/image_cache/...` path in installs where the legacy dir exists. I reproduced it today on Linux/Telegram DM:
    
    -  did not show as an attachment.
    - Retrying did not show.
    - `send_message` reported success but still did not visibly deliver.
    - Copying the same PNG to `/root/.hermes/image_cache/...` and sending from there worked reliably.
    
    PR #31764 looks like the right fix direction: explicitly add canonical cache roots like `cache/images`, `cache/audio`, `cache/videos`, etc. to `MEDIA_DELIVERY_SAFE_ROOTS`.
    
    Only concern: the PR is currently conflicting, and the tests mostly monkeypatch `MEDIA_DELIVERY_SAFE_ROOTS`, so they don’t fully prove the real default safe roots include `cache/images` when legacy `image_cache` also exists.
    
    My take: correct fix direction. Best path is probably to get #31764 rebased and strengthened rather than opening a duplicate PR. If it stalls, I’m happy to help put up a clean replacement PR that credits #31764.
    [embed] Generated images under cache/images may not deliver via Telegram ME... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/31733
    [embed] fix(gateway): add canonical cache paths to MEDIA_DELIVERY_SAFE_ROOT... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/31764

[2026-05-26T21:40:17.460000+00:00] sprmn24 (id=1508947913437614261)
    <@387972437901312000> , I opened a few PRs today — take a look when you get a chance:
    
    **fix(agent): wrap list-type untrusted content in untrusted_tool_result**
    `web_extract` and `browser_*` tools can return multimodal content lists instead of plain strings. `_maybe_wrap_untrusted()` was skipping the `<untrusted_tool_result>` wrapper entirely for list-type content — any injection payload inside a text chunk reached the model unguarded. Fixed by wrapping each `{"type": "text"}` part individually while leaving image parts untouched.
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32771
    
    **fix(tools): fall back to Python read on Windows where head is unavailable**
    `_detect_file_line_ending()` uses `head -c 4096` to sample file content, but `head` doesn't exist on Windows. On a local backend + Windows setup this always failed silently, returning `None` and disabling CRLF preservation for every write. Added a `platform.system() == "Windows"` check that falls back to Python's built-in `open().read(4096)`.
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32789
    
    **fix(tools): clamp patch failure counter to 10 to prevent unbounded growth**
    `_record_patch_failure()` increments a per-file failure counter with no upper bound. In long sessions the counter grows indefinitely, producing misleading escalation messages. Clamped to 10 with `min(count, 10)` — the escalation hint already triggers at 3 so no caller needs a higher value.
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32792
    
    **fix(agent): call scan_for_threats on untrusted tool results to log promptware hits**
    `tools/threat_patterns.py` is documented as "shared with the tool-result delimiter system" but `tool_dispatch_helpers.py` never imported or called `scan_for_threats()`. Known C2, role-hijack and promptware patterns passed through silently with no log entry. Wired the missing call with a guarded import; matches are logged at WARNING level.
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32793
    
    **fix(env_loader): add threading.Lock to _APPLIED_HOMES to prevent TOCTOU race**
    The idempotency guard in `_apply_external_secret_sources()` uses a bare check-then-set on a module-level set with no lock. Two threads calling `load_hermes_dotenv()` concurrently can both pass the `in` check before either executes the `add`, causing the Bitwarden CLI to be invoked twice simultaneously. Wrapped the guard in `threading.Lock()`.
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32796

[2026-05-26T22:02:55.715000+00:00] kkurottal6611 (id=1508953610371858653)

    #32807 (File Sync Python 3.11): Fixed sync_back() crashes on Python 3.11 setups caused by unconditional 3.12-only filter="data" extraction calls. Added safe member extraction fallback + sorted out cross-platform temp locks. https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32807

[2026-05-27T13:21:50.617000+00:00] godsboy7777 (id=1509184862911991878)
    <@387972437901312000> Follow-up on yesterday's MEDIA delivery question, Issue #31733 / PR #31764.
    
    The bug surface is broader than the generated-image cache root mismatch.
    
    Repro'd today with a valid PDF under `/tmp` on Linux / Telegram DM, screenshot attached above:
    
    1. Agent confirms the PDF exists at `/tmp/pool_evidence/Cosmo_Pools_Quotation_PFA30971.pdf`.
    2. Agent's final response says "Here it is" with a `MEDIA:<path>` tag. No attachment appears in Telegram.
    3. I reply, "There is no pdf here?" because the text shows but the file does not.
    4. Agent recovers by running `mkdir -p /root/.hermes/document_cache`, copying the PDF in, and invoking `send_message` against the new path. Telegram delivers it natively.
    5. The bot's own self-narration nails the diagnosis: "Telegram didn't attach it from the /tmp path. I copied it into the document cache and sent it directly."
    
    The gateway log for step 2 today just says:
    
    ```text
    Skipping unsafe MEDIA directive path outside allowed roots
    ```
    
    with no path and no reason, so the agent has nothing to act on except blind retry to a different directory.
    
    Per yesterday's offer, I have opened #33251 as a complementary PR to #31764. It is ready for review and CI is green, 18/18.
    
    PR: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/33251
    Issue: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/31733
    Related PR: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/31764
    Walkthrough comment on screenshot: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/33251#issuecomment-4554794390
    
    ## What #33251 adds on top of #31764's canonical-cache-roots direction
    
    * Kebab-string rejection reasons surfaced from the validator and logged by `filter_media_delivery_paths` / `filter_local_delivery_paths` with a redacted path.
    * The `/tmp` PDF case in the screenshot would log:
    
    ```text
    Skipping unsafe MEDIA directive path /tmp/.../Cosmo_Pools_Quotation_PFA30971.pdf, reason=outside-allowlist-stale-mtime
    ```
    
    That is actionable for operators, via Grafana / log search, and for the agent if or when we surface the reason back into the tool return value. I am tracking that as a deferred follow-up.
    
    * Control-character sanitisation in the redacted log, so a model-emitted path with an embedded newline cannot forge a second log line.
    * The strengthened legacy-and-canonical co-exist regression test the #31764 review asked for.
    * Legacy `image_cache/` and canonical `cache/images/` both exist on disk.
    * The canonical file is deliverable through the default safe-roots tuple, no monkeypatch.
    * PDF / document delivery regression tests under canonical `cache/documents` and legacy `document_cache`.
    * `send_message` / final-response extraction parity verified via source introspection, so a future split surfaces in tests.
    
    Scope is intentionally narrow: gateway media delivery only.
    
    It does not touch the extension-derivation refactor, #32472, or the markdown attachment work, #31561 / #33206.
    
    If #31764 lands first, the safe-roots block in #33251 is a clean merge against it.
    
    Which path do you prefer?
    
    1. Take #33251 as the consolidated PR and close or supersede #31764.
    2. Fold the diagnosable-logging additions and the strengthened tests from #33251 into #31764, and I will close #33251.
    
    Happy either way. Whichever lands faster and easier for the maintainers.

[2026-05-27T15:47:02.325000+00:00] ophermcar00_70039 (id=1509221402463568014)
    Hey all — I’m debugging a Hermes Agent / Telegram issue and I think I’ve narrowed it to the Python OpenAI Codex response parser.
    
    Setup:
    
    * Hermes Agent running locally on macOS
    * Process: `python -m hermes_cli.main gateway run --replace`
    * State/config: `~/.hermes`
    * Telegram bot: IGotHermesBot
    * Provider: `openai-codex`
    * Model: `gpt-5.4`
    * Codex OAuth is valid and refreshed
    
    What works:
    
    * Hermes gateway starts cleanly
    * Telegram slash commands work
    * Codex OAuth is present and valid
    * Clean/new session was tested
    * The request reaches `https://chatgpt.com/backend-api/codex/responses`
    * HTTP call completes in ~3–5 seconds
    
    What fails:
    
    * Any normal Telegram message fails with:
      `The model provider failed after retries`
      and:
      `TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not iterable`
    
    What we ruled out:
    
    * Not OpenClaw — separate stack, OpenClaw/HauseBot works
    * Not Telegram connectivity — slash commands work
    * Not Codex auth — OAuth is valid
    * Not poisoned session state — deleted/exported the broken session and tested clean history
    * Not model id — changed from `gpt-5.4-mini` to `gpt-5.4`; request now shows `model: gpt-5.4`, same failure
    
    Latest evidence:
    
    * Fresh session with `history=0`
    * Single user message: `Reply with only: OK`
    * Model: `gpt-5.4`
    * Codex backend request completes
    * Hermes throws `TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not iterable`
    * Request dump only stores request + error summary, not raw response or traceback
    
    Question:
    Where in Hermes should I inspect or patch the OpenAI Codex Responses client/parser to capture the raw response/SSE events and full traceback safely? It looks like the backend is returning a response shape Hermes doesn’t handle, possibly `output: null` or empty/malformed events.
    
    I can provide redacted logs/request dump details, but I do not want to expose OAuth tokens, Telegram token, cookies, auth headers, or full config.

[2026-05-27T15:59:23.992000+00:00] thadreber (id=1509224513240432843)
    BadHost (CVE-2026-48710) — Starlette < 1.0.1                                                                                                                                       
    Single-character Host header injection bypasses path-based auth in Starlette. Trivial to exploit, no auth required. Affects anything built on FastAPI/Starlette — vLLM,     LiteLLM, MCP servers, agent harnesses, eval dashboards.                                                                                                                            
    This includes us — Hermes runs Starlette. Just upgraded our venvs from 1.0.0 → 1.1.0.                                                                                              
    If you're running any Python AI infra on Starlette, check your version and upgrade:                                                                                                
                                                                                                                                                                                            
     pip install 'starlette>=1.0.1'                                                                                                                                                     
                                                                                                                                                                                            
     Online scanner: https://badhost.org                                                                                                                                                
     Advisory: https://www.secwest.net/starlette                                                                                                                                        
    X41 writeup: https://www.x41-dsec.de/lab/advisories/x41-2026-002-starlette
    [embed] BadHost - CVE-2026-48710 Starlette Host-Header Auth Bypass https://badhost.org/
    [embed] starlette  - secwest.net - secure virtual engagement https://www.secwest.net/starlette
    [embed] Advisory X41-2026-002: Request Host Header not Validated in Starlette https://www.x41-dsec.de/lab/advisories/x41-2026-002-starlette

[2026-05-27T19:20:58.591000+00:00] lito9596 (id=1509275241665532126)
    Two PRs addressing #30565 (toolsets silently disappearing when env vars are only in ~/.hermes/.env):
    
    **#30580 — Diagnostic logging for filtered toolsets**
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/30580
    Adds a one-shot WARNING per filtered toolset naming the missing env vars. Deduped per process. Already labeled by @alt-glitch.
    
    **#33423 — Root-cause fix: invalidate tool caches on dotenv reload**
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/33423
    `load_hermes_dotenv()` reloads `.env` but never invalidated `_check_fn_cache` or `_tool_defs_cache`, so stale availability results persisted across reloads. This clears both caches after every dotenv reload. Root-cause trace with file:line evidence posted on the issue: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/30565#issuecomment-4557380468
    
    Both PRs have passing tests and no conflicts with main.
    [embed] fix(tools): warn when toolsets are filtered by missing env vars by ... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/30580
    [embed] fix(tools): invalidate tool caches on dotenv reload by pnascimento9... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/33423
    [embed] discord/discord_admin tools silently hidden when DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN ... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/30565

[2026-05-28T18:49:54.269000+00:00] godsboy7777 (id=1509629809997910078)
    <@387972437901312000> quick one for review when you have a gap.
    
    I noticed the bundled `baoyu-infographic` skill could be interpreted loosely enough that an agent may create a scripted/handmade infographic instead of actually prompting the configured Hermes `image_generate` tool.
    
    In our case, Hermes image gen was correctly configured to:
    - provider: `openai-codex`
    - model: `gpt-image-2-high`
    
    …but the skill guidance was not strict enough to prevent silent substitution when the agent tried to improve text readability.
    
    Issue:
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/34046
    
    PR:
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/34047
    
    PR summary:
    - Clarifies that `baoyu-infographic` must use the Hermes `image_generate` tool with the assembled prompt.
    - States that the configured Hermes image-generation backend should not be bypassed silently.
    - Adds guidance that if exact readable text needs HTML/SVG/Pillow overlay, the agent should tell the user before switching away from image gen.
    - Updates both the skill and bundled docs mirror.
    
    CI so far:
    - `check-common-ancestor` passed.
    - Docs/Nix checks were still pending when I last checked.
    
    This is a small docs/skill-instruction fix, but I think it prevents a confusing user-facing behaviour.
    [embed] [Bug]: baoyu infographic skill should not silently bypass image_gen... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/34046
    [embed] fix(skills): clarify infographic image generation by GodsBoy · Pul... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/34047

[2026-05-28T22:28:00.331000+00:00] promptsiren (id=1509684696919834725)
    PR: <https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/34131>
    Cluster: **execute_code sandbox bypasses approval and leaks secrets**
    7 active items (4 issues, 3 PRs) / agent, environments, gateway, hermes_cli
    Reported theme: security vulnerabilities in the execute_code functionality where dangerous commands bypass the manual approval guard and sensitive environment variables can be leaked.
    
    - Issue [#4146](<https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/4146>) Critical: Sandbox code execution bypasses dangerous command approval via terminal tool
    - Issue [#27303](<https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/27303>) execute_code can leak common secret env vars and bypass terminal approval guard
    - PR [#27304](<https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/27304>) fix: harden execute_code env and approvals
    - Issue [#30882](<https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/30882>) [Bug]: [Security] approvals.mode=manual silently bypassed in gateway mode — dangerous commands execute without approval, including via execute_code sandbox evasion
    - PR [#30893](<https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/30893>) [codex] Guard execute_code behind gateway approvals
    - Issue [#33057](<https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/issues/33057>) fix(cli): dangerous command approval bypass in execute_code RPC threads
    - PR [#33246](<https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/33246>) [codex] Preserve context in execute_code RPC threads
    
    Root cause: execute_code's RPC worker threads ran as bare threads, dropping the gateway session ContextVar (dangerous commands hit the non-interactive auto-approve path) and the thread-local approval callbacks.
    • Shared context+callback propagation into both RPC threads — supersedes #33246 (ContextVar-only); dedupes the existing tool_executor onto it
    • One-shot fail-closed entry guard before a script spawns — adapts #30893
    • Env scrub: broad `HERMES_` passthrough → explicit allowlist + DSN/WEBHOOK blocking — covers #27303/#27304
    • Gateway startup warning for bare manual-approval configs

[2026-05-29T07:17:57.527000+00:00] godsboy7777 (id=1509818064026472558)
    <@387972437901312000> could you take a look at PR #33251 when you get a chance?
    
    It is the MEDIA delivery diagnosability + canonical cache roots fix. It is now rebased cleanly on top of #34022 and mergeable.
    
    ## Why it still matters after the recent denylist-only change
    
    #34022 flipped the default to denylist-only, which restored stale / outside delivery and fixed the most visible "MEDIA didn't attach" reports.
    
    But three gaps are still live on `main` right now:
    
    ## 1. Rejections are still undiagnosable
    
    `filter_media_delivery_paths` still logs the blind message:
    
    ```text
    Skipping unsafe MEDIA directive path outside allowed roots
    ```
    
    with no path and no reason.
    
    This PR logs a redacted path plus a kebab reason tag for both default and strict modes, for example:
    
    ```text
    denylist-cleared
    outside-allowlist-stale-mtime
    does-not-resolve
    ```
    
    That is the exact gap the Linux Telegram repro and @shashb27's Windows repro both hit:
    
    * Reply looks successful
    * Attachment never lands
    * Log says nothing actionable
    
    ## 2. Live batch-abort bug
    
    A model-emitted `~\x00...` path raises `ValueError` in `os.path.expanduser` before `.resolve()`.
    
    The filter loops have no per-item guard, so one bad path silently drops every attachment in the response.
    
    Reproduced against current `main`. This PR guards it.
    
    ## 3. Canonical cache roots still bite in strict mode
    
    #31733 is still open, and #31764 / #33238 are unmerged.
    
    This PR adds the canonical roots so generated images under:
    
    ```text
    ~/.hermes/cache/images
    ```
    
    deliver under strict mode too.
    
    It also adds U+2028 / U+2029 / NEL log-forging hardening, which does not exist on `main` at all.
    
    ## Why this is the right shape
    
    This is the broadest of the canonical-roots PRs. #31764 and #33238 are narrower subsets.
    
    It complements #34022 rather than fighting it.
    
    It is also orthogonal to:
    
    * #32472
    * #31561 / #33206
    * The separate #23760 / #18686 `_send_via_adapter` drop
    
    So it does not block or overlap any of them.
    
    ## Tests
    
    ```text
    tests/gateway/test_platform_base.py
    136 passing
    ```
    
    Broader gateway + scheduler suite:
    
    ```text
    6127 passing
    0 failed
    ```
    
    Happy to split the hardening into its own PR if you'd rather land the diagnosability piece first.

[2026-05-29T12:23:46.702000+00:00] godsboy7777 (id=1509895026044309554)
    Hey <@387972437901312000>, I know I have flagged this one before, so sorry for circling back.
    
    The reason I am raising it again is that it is in a genuinely different state now: fully green and mergeable, so it should be a one-click merge whenever you have a window.
    
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/26286
    
    ## What changed since the last ping
    
    * Rebased cleanly against current `main`
    * Previous conflict is resolved
    * All CI checks now pass
    * Focused web/tool suite is green: **268 tests**
    * GitHub reports it as **CLEAN / mergeable**
    
    ## What it adds
    
    A first-class paid Brave Search API backend alongside the existing free `brave-free` fallback.
    
    Fully additive and opt-in.
    
    ### Highlights
    
    * Adds a direct `brave_search` tool for agents with richer modes:
    
      * Web
      * LLM grounding
      * Images
      * News
      * Videos
      * Discussions
      * Suggest
      * Bounded raw
    
    * Avoids routing everything through one generic `web_search` shape.
    
    * Default `both` mode returns web results plus Brave LLM grounding context in one call, so agents get source URLs and compact grounding snippets together.
    
    * Backward compatible:
    
      * Existing `BRAVE_SEARCH_API_KEY` users stay on `brave-free`
      * Users only switch if they explicitly pick `brave-search`
    
    * Clean plugin integration under:
    
    ```text
    plugins/web/brave_search/
    ```
    
    * Registered web-toolset tool
    * Docs and env-var coverage included
    
    No rush, and totally fine to squash-push or adopt it however is easiest.
    
    Just wanted to flag that it is finally in a ready, mergeable state.

[2026-05-29T14:11:35.723000+00:00] promptsiren (id=1509922159084961802)
    Cluster: **Silent response loss after agent tool calls**
    6 active items (3 issues, 3 PRs) / agent, agent/transports, gateway, hermes_cli
    Reported theme: agent responses silently dropped or frozen after tool calls complete, with no output delivered to the user.
    
    Root cause: this is two unrelated bugs plus one already-fixed issue. The #29346 drops are gateway-side: a streamed preamble finalized at a tool boundary marks the turn delivered, so the gateway suppresses the genuine answer the next API call produces; and the extract pipeline can reduce a non-empty response to empty text with no attachment, after which the `if text_content` send guard skips delivery with no log. #28834 is a separate CLI bug, and #7968 was already fixed on main.
    • Scope the stream consumer's final-delivery flags to the turn-final segment, cleared at every tool boundary, so a preamble can no longer suppress the real answer (duplicate-safe by construction)
    • Recover extract-stripped responses on every platform, taken from the post-extract body so a spaced MEDIA path can't leak — supersedes #33842 (Discord-only) and #29499 (unsafe raw fallback)
    • Loud delivery invariant (response_delivery_recovered / response_delivery_dropped) so a non-empty response no longer vanishes silently — closes #29346
    • #28834/#29004 — a separate bug (tool-call freeze after idle; CLI-side, not gateway delivery), not addressed here. The keepalive approach in #29004 won't fix it (httpx already recycles the idle socket at its 5s expiry; the socket-option change is a no-op on the reporter's Python 3.9) — but we couldn't reproduce the freeze to pin the real cause, so #28834 stays open with a repro protocol
    • #7968 already fixed on main by #10472 — ticket just needs closing
    
    PR: https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/34336

[2026-05-29T14:55:27.369000+00:00] promptsiren (id=1509933197008441437)
    Cluster: **Gateway MEDIA regex missing file extension support**
    19 active items (6 issues, 13 PRs) / gateway
    Reported theme: The gateway's MEDIA extraction regex/allowlist is missing or dropping certain file extensions (notably .md and .html), causing attachments of those types to be silently ignored or fail to send.
    
    Root cause: the gateway grew ~5 independent copies of "what counts as a deliverable file" (extract_media, extract_local_files, the two run.py tool-dedup sites, stream_consumer, and the dispatch strip), and they drifted. Commit ea49b3862 tightened extract_media into a hard, case-sensitive allowlist that dropped .md/.html, and the other copies never matched it — so .md/.html, uppercase, bold-wrapped, and spaced paths silently failed to attach while send_message still reported success. A second, related silent failure: a MEDIA path outside the delivery roots was filtered out while the text still sent, so the tool reported success with no attachment (#32644).
    • One curated _DELIVERY_MEDIA_EXTS frozenset + shared compiled regexes collapse the ~5 drifted copies onto a single source of truth; broaden delivery to .md/.html, uppercase, bold/quoted/spaced paths, dedup, and GIS (.kml/.kmz/.geojson/.gpx) — consolidates ~30 stalled extension PRs and closes the extension-drift issues
    • Recognize Windows (C:\, C:/) and UNC (\\server\share) MEDIA: paths so the raw path is stripped from the message instead of leaking as plain text (the #28989 / #24032 symptom) — delivery of those still fail-closes
    • Loud media_dropped result so a path outside the delivery roots is reported instead of a silent text-only success — closes the silent-success half of #32644, supersedes #34178
    • Source/script/config exts (.py/.sh/.toml/.log/…) stay excluded — the curated set is the union of what was already deliverable; auto-delivering those outbound is a separate product decision (out of scope)
    • Out of scope / future work: actually delivering files from new locations — native-Windows delivery and safe-roots honoring — touches the path-validation exfiltration boundary, so it's left for a separate security-reviewed change; the delivery halves of #24032 / #28989 / #32644 stay open
    
    PR: <https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/34656>

[2026-05-29T16:10:52.542000+00:00] .blockops (id=1509952176959520788)
    Hello all, I've seen some folks post about this:
    https://github.com/fathah/hermes-desktop
    
    I think this is a great way to make Hermes more approachable for the standard user but I'm concerned about the threat model. As far as I can tell this dev has no affiliation with Nous Research. He made a nice electron control plane for Hermes. The implementation is fairly decent Electron hardening. I don't love the code signing, admin password handling (temp scripts, sudo shims to path, unix socket server that receives passwords through the socket then returns to sudo... socket is chmodded 0600 and dialog is reasonably hardened through sandboxing and strict CSP but the password flow through electron's app process still gives me worry), the access to secrets from Electron and the automatic updates (given the amount of supply chain attacks we're seeing these days). 
    
    I'd love to see Nous potentially take over a project similar to this one to make Hermes even more approachable and be able to trust that it's supported by the core dev team and not some third party dev. 
    
    Love to hear Nous's teams feedback on it.
    [embed] GitHub - fathah/hermes-desktop: Desktop Companion for Hermes Agent https://github.com/fathah/hermes-desktop

[2026-05-29T23:28:50.350000+00:00] promptsiren (id=1510062394074730729)
    **PR #35028 Gateway working-directory fix**
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35028
    Root cause: the system prompt emitted `os.getcwd()` (daemon launch dir) instead of the configured `TERMINAL_CWD`, so the agent `cd`'d to the wrong place (gateway/cron/Telegram). Fix: one reader resolver `agent/runtime_cwd.py` as single source of truth, routing the 2 prompt read sites through it. Reader-only; behavior-preserving for local-CLI + remote backends.
    Closes #24882 #24969 #27383 · supersedes PRs #24888 #24985 #27488 #29365 — dropped their per-session "re-bridge" (TERMINAL_CWD is never lost, so it patched a non-bug; refs #10817 #10225). #11312 is a separate service-manager env-loss issue (→ landed #34805), not closed here.
    3 core + 4 test files · 146 passed, 1 skipped · ruff clean.

[2026-05-29T23:29:08.242000+00:00] promptsiren (id=1510062469119213679)
    ===============================
    **PR #35033 — `delegate_task` per-task model/provider overrides**
    <https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35033>
    Adds `tasks[].model` / `tasks[].provider` (precedence: per-task > `delegation.*` config > parent). Also fixes 2 latent bugs that silently made `delegation.*` inert:
    • **A:** `_load_config` swallowed config-load failures silently → now warns.
    • **B:** a child pinned to explicit `delegation.base_url` got a credential pool whose `_swap_credential` redirected it off-endpoint → pool now skipped for literal config endpoints (provider-resolved keeps key rotation).
    
    Tight scope: `delegate_tool.py` + tests only (+245/−11, 7 commits, 146+17 green). No top-level `model=` arg by design — that surface was deliberately removed upstream (fb0f579b1).
    Closes #7833 #15789 #17685 #18591 #12440 #27294 #27295 #27296

[2026-05-30T00:59:12.566000+00:00] promptsiren (id=1510085136497119372)
    PR #35056 **Custom-provider fallback fix**
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35056
    Root cause behind 6 issues: provider/credential resolution lived in parallel paths (CLI/gateway/ACP/aux) that had drifted, all terminating in OpenRouter → a configured custom/local provider got silently dropped to OpenRouter (or credential-mixed).
    **Fix:** one offline resolver → one `ResolvedProvider` → one alias table → one `/v1` normalizer → one path-scoped api_mode detector; **fail-closed** on custom intent; zero network during resolve.
    **Closes** #3263 #4600 #5358 #8919 #12146 #13489 · **supersedes** #17072 · GHSA-76xc-57q6-vm5m host-gating preserved + hardened.
    26 files (11 core / 15 test), 5 semantic commits.
    ⚠️ **Behavior:** `OPENAI_BASE_URL` no longer routes (warn-only) · ambient OPENAI/OPENROUTER keys no longer override a configured provider · bare `custom` with no base_url+key now errors instead of silently using OpenRouter.

[2026-05-30T21:26:25.477000+00:00] ahmetarif0x (id=1510393975310258247)
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35525
    get_nous_auth_status() had no threading lock on its process-level cache. Concurrent callers on a cold cache all called _compute_nous_auth_status() simultaneously — a synchronous OAuth refresh POST (~350ms) that burns single-use Nous refresh tokens. Added _NOUS_AUTH_STATUS_CACHE_LOCK, mirroring the identical fix applied to _account_info_cache in PR #35337.
    
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35528
    check_fal_api_key(), _has_openai_audio_backend(), and _check_fal_video_available() are called by the tool/model picker on every UI render. All three called resolve_managed_tool_gateway() which defaults to read_nous_access_token — a synchronous OAuth refresh POST on every availability scan. Switched to is_managed_tool_gateway_ready() which uses peek_nous_access_token instead. Parity with PR #35401 (browser-use + firecrawl).
    
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35531
    _apply_yaml_config() in the Mattermost adapter translated require_mention, free_response_channels, and allowed_channels from config.yaml to env vars — but was missing allow_from → MATTERMOST_ALLOWED_USERS. Users who set mattermost.allow_from in config.yaml got no user filtering; the bot responded to everyone. Parity with PR #35329 (Discord allow_from bridge).
    
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35533
    In tui_gateway/server.py, /compress here 3 passed "here 3" directly as focus_topic to the full compress — the boundary-aware split was silently ignored. Added parse_partial_compress_args() parsing to mirror cli.py and gateway/run.py's /compress here [N] implementation from PR #35252.
    [embed] fix(auth): add threading lock to get_nous_auth_status() cache by Ah... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35525
    [embed] fix(tools): use is_managed_tool_gateway_ready in FAL/TTS availabili... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35528
    [embed] fix(mattermost): bridge allow_from config key to MATTERMOST_ALLOWED... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35531
    [embed] fix(tui-gateway): parse partial compress args in /compress here [N]... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35533

[2026-05-31T00:57:36.047000+00:00] frowtek (id=1510447119532556308)
    What does this PR do?
    Fixes legacy messaging setup flows that tell operators "leave empty for open access" but do not actually persist an open-access flag. At runtime, those platforms then fall back to DM pairing or denial behavior instead of the access mode the wizard promised.
    
    The fix centralizes DM access-policy setup into a shared helper and updates the affected setup flows so a blank allowlist now explicitly branches into one of two persisted modes:
    
    open access: writes the platform-scoped *_ALLOW_ALL_USERS=true
    DM pairing: writes the platform-scoped *_ALLOW_ALL_USERS=false
    When an explicit allowlist is provided, the setup now also forces *_ALLOW_ALL_USERS=false so stale open-access state cannot survive a reconfiguration.
    
    https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35608
    [embed] fix(setup): persist open access vs pairing for blank allowlists by ... https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/35608

[2026-05-31T18:34:12.312000+00:00] rfv370_65340 (id=1510713022740562050)
    **Hermes update governance pattern — feedback welcome**
    
    Hi all,
    Sharing an operational note from a long-lived Hermes deployment.
    I recently audited our hermes' weekly update process and found that a direct scheduled `hermes update` path could hit `approval_required`, while our governed maintenance path worked reliably.
    
    The working pattern is:
    1. Policy/governance check
    2. Runtime backup/checkpoint
    3. Off-host backup push
    4. Hermes update
    5. Version/status verification
    6. Rollback point preserved
    
    This seems like a useful pattern for long-running Hermes installs with external integrations, SSH access, email, etc.
    
    Curious how others handle this:
    * Is there a recommended Nous pattern for backup → update → verify → rollback?
    * Are others using systemd/wrapper-based maintenance flows?
    * Is a native `hermes maintenance` workflow something already discussed?
    
    Not a bug report — just sharing field experience and checking whether we are aligned with existing best practice.
    Let me know if I am not in the right thread.

[2026-06-01T00:33:48.947000+00:00] frowtek (id=1510803521707049161)
    What does this PR do?
    Fixes a lost /restart confirmation. The requester's routing info is saved to
    .restart_notify.json and delivered once at startup. If the requester's platform
    adapter hadn't reconnected yet, _send_restart_notification() returned early but
    the finally block still deleted the marker, and the reconnect watcher never
    retried — so the "Gateway restarted successfully" message promised in the
    slash-command docs was permanently lost on a late reconnect.
